channel.title: NYT > Arts > Art & Design
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: ‘Greater New York’ Brings the Noisy, Messy Vitality of 53 Artists
item.description: The signature survey by MoMA PS 1 of artists living and working in the city highlights those whose talent is often hidden in plain sight.
item.dc:creator: Max Lakin and Vincent Tullo
item.media:credit: Vincent Tullo for The New York Times

item.title: The Phillies Owner and His Wife Collect Art’s Heavy Hitters
item.description: Mets fans, avert your eyes: John Middleton, majority owner of the Phillies, and his wife have a deep bench of American art stars, and they’ve lent them in a dual display for the 250th.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Leigh Brown and Michelle Gustafson
item.category: Pennsylvania Academy of the Fine Arts
item.media:description: Leigh Middleton and her husband, John Middleton, a.k.a. the secretive collector curators called “Mr. Big,” with “Child and Boat” by Edmund Charles Tarbell (1899).

item.title: As Los Angeles Rebuilds, a Surge of Experimentation
item.description: Across the city’s fire zones, there’s a surge of experimentation — collective rebuilding, catalog homes and new technologies that are safe and reduce costs.
item.dc:creator: Sam Lubell and Carlos Jaramillo
item.media:description: An empty home lot overlooking the Pacific after the fires, one of 16,000 homes destroyed. Challenges to rebuilding are stiff: labor shortages, tariff-induced cost spikes, lower-than-expected insurance payouts, and slow-moving bureaucracies. Cue the innovators.

item.title: Daring and Dazzling, a New LACMA Floats Above Los Angeles
item.description: After $724 million and a decade of battles, the pugnacious David Geffen Galleries reassert the city’s role as a petri dish for experimental design.
item.dc:creator: Michael Kimmelman and Jake Michaels
item.category: Los Angeles County Museum of Art
item.media:credit: Jake Michaels for The New York Times

item.title: A Thomas J Price Bronze Opens Door to London’s V&A East
item.description: Thomas J Price’s bronze figures present anonymous Black people at heroic scale. After an installation in Times Square sparked a furor, his latest work welcomes visitors to a new museum outpost.
item.dc:creator: Eleanor Stanford
item.media:credit: Ayesha Kazim for The New York Times
item.media:description: Price’s sculpture “A Place Beyond,” stands outside the new V&A East in London, which opens Saturday.

item.title: A Sculptor’s Life, in Constant Motion
item.description: A career-spanning Alexander Calder exhibition in Paris turns the viewer into a collaborator and lifts the soul.
item.dc:creator: Emily LaBarge and Dmitry Kostyukov
item.media:credit: Dmitry Kostyukov for The New York Times; Artworks: 2025 Calder Foundation, New York/ADAGP, Paris, 2026

item.title: Paris Man Wins $1.2 Million Picasso Painting in Charity Raffle
item.description: A Parisian software salesman entered a charity raffle and came away with a piece of history: “I have some paintings, but not like a Picasso.”
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor

item.title: What’s That Musky Aroma at the New Museum?
item.description: The artist Klara Hosnedlova inaugurates OMA’s soaring new atrium stairway at the New Museum.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Leigh Brown
item.media:credit: Sabrina Santiago for The New York Times

item.title: Art Gallery Shows to See in April
item.description: This week in Newly Reviewed, Andrew Russeth covers Édouard Vuillard’s thorny encounters, Domenico Gnoli’s zesty paintings and Nina Beier’s ice cream cones.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Russeth, Jillian Steinhauer and Travis Diehl

item.title: Wisconsin’s Chazen Museum of Art Explores New Ways to Display Its Collection
item.description: At the Chazen Museum of Art in Wisconsin, each gallery will now have a single “focus object,” with “a constellation of other artworks” helping to draw out particular themes.
item.dc:creator: Ted Loos

item.title: After the Heists: Securing Museums Without Closing Them Off
item.description: The bold robbery at the Louvre shocked the world, but all museums face the challenge of protecting art and historical treasures.
item.dc:creator: Sam Lubell
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: A variety of new walls step along the sloping site of the Norton Simon Museum, including a textured white concrete barrier in various segments, to the east and a matte white wall to the west.

item.title: A Dance Party for Martha Graham With Ariana DeBose and Padma Lakshmi
item.description: Stars and luminaries honored the dance company’s 100th anniversary with dinner, performances and a champagne toast at the New York Public Library.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr and Rebecca Smeyne
item.category: Graham, Martha, Center of Contemporary Dance
New York Public Library for the Performing Arts

item.title: Paul Waldman, Creator and Curator of Art Museum for Birds, Dies at 89
item.description: A dyslexic teenager, he reinvented himself as a bodybuilder. Then he turned to art, producing transgressive paintings and elaborate birdhouses.
item.dc:creator: Michael S. Rosenwald
item.media:credit: Michael Shavel for The New York Times
item.media:description: Paul Waldman in 1993 with the International Bird Museum that he built on the roof of his house in Southampton, N.Y. The multistory birdhouse had an art gallery that displayed miniature works by Roy Lichtenstein and others.

item.title: At Milan Design Week, Noodling Around With an Italian Classic
item.description: An exhibition of 3-D-printed pasta reveals what could be the most disruptive culinary innovation since the eggbeater.
item.dc:creator: Ian Volner

item.title: Flowery Films Whose Plant Life Lingers in the Mind
item.description: Ten artists and designers reflect on the onscreen flora, from fantastical topiaries to scene-setting bouquets, that’s inspired them.
item.dc:creator: Laura Regensdorf

item.title: Barber Osgerby, a British Design Team, Is Honored in Milan
item.description: The Triennale design museum is hosting an exhibition of work by the renowned studio Barber Osgerby.
item.dc:creator: Bridget Goldberg

item.title: Celeste Dupuy-Spencer, Painter of Hotly Topical Images, Dies at 46
item.description: Her vibrant yet brooding work explored the crises of the Trump era, with paintings depicting the Jan. 6 riots and a mangled Confederate monument.
item.dc:creator: Alex Williams

item.title: Metropolitan Opera and MoMA Together Put Kahlo at Center Stage
item.description: In an unusual collaboration for the Met, the opera’s set designer has conceived a companion exhibition, mounted at MoMA.
item.dc:creator: Hilarie M. Sheets

item.title: Recalling When Lower Manhattan Was New Amsterdam
item.description: An exhibition at the New York Historical focuses on the city’s 17th-century roots as a Dutch settlement.
item.dc:creator: Megan McCrea
item.media:credit: Shuran Huang for The New York Times
item.media:description: Russell Shorto, the director of the New Amsterdam Project at the New York Historical, walking in Lower Manhattan and explaining the history of the Dutch settlement.

item.title: At the Guggenheim, Pop Art Engages With Art of the Present
item.description: An exhibition explores examples of Pop Art from the 1960s in dialogue with recent acquisitions by contemporary artists.
item.dc:creator: Robin Pogrebin
item.media:credit: Colin Clark for The New York Times
item.media:description: Lauren Hinkson, the Guggenheim Museum curator who organized the show “Guggenheim Pop: 1960 to Now,” with examples of the art in the show.

item.title: Pittsburgh’s Children’s Museum Satisfies a Hunger for Eric Carle
item.description: The institution will feature five of the beloved author’s collage-based books in a series of interactive exhibits meant to engage children.
item.dc:creator: Shivani Vora
item.media:credit: Ross Mantle for The New York Times
item.media:description: Preschoolers from Pittsburgh Public Schools hiding in and weaving through a field of four-foot-tall blades of grass in “The Very Clumsy Click Beetle” section of “Very Eric Carle.”

item.title: A ‘Cabinet of Wonders’ on Show at the American Academy of Arts and Letters
item.description: Current members of the museum have created a show that draws from, and comments on, the institution’s curious collections.
item.dc:creator: Laurel Graeber
item.category: American Academy of Arts and Letters

item.title: In Its ‘Greater New York’ Show, MoMA PS1 Focuses on the Here and Now
item.description: Even as the institution has grown and changed, it has continued to be a launchpad for emerging artists. This spring, it is putting 53 in the spotlight.
item.dc:creator: Leslie Wayne
item.media:credit: Daniel Terna for The New York Times
item.media:description: A mural titled “Greater New York” (2026) greets visitors to the exhibition. It was created by the Cevallos Brothers, who have been making colorful signs and posters for Latin American stores in Queens for decades.

item.title: The Aldrich Contemporary Art Museum Puts Connecticut Artists in the Spotlight
item.description: The Aldrich Contemporary Art Museum in Ridgefield will showcase works by people who live and work in New York’s shadow.
item.dc:creator: Annabel Keenan
item.media:credit: Christopher Capozziello for The New York Times
item.media:description: Lula Mae Blocton of Eastern Connecticut State University works on a painting in her home studio.

item.title: In the Tech Heart of Texas, an Art Show Built on Data, Code and A.I.
item.description: The showcase features works that change from hour to hour, invite interaction and interrogate the idea of creativity itself.
item.dc:creator: Jillian Anthony
item.category: Blanton, Jack S, Museum of Art

item.title: Honoring Frederic Church: Beyond the Hudson River School
item.description: His many achievements have been obscured, some believe, by his reputation as a provincial landscape painter.
item.dc:creator: Hilarie M. Sheets
item.media:credit: via The Olana Partnership
item.media:description: “Our Banner in the Sky” (1861) will be included in the exhibition “Frederic Church: Global Artist,” opening May 17 at the Olana State Historic Site.

item.title: ‘Rethinking, Reimagining and Reinstalling’ the Metropolitan Museum of Art
item.description: A $1.5 billion project will transform the nation’s most-visited art museum, with renovations involving a quarter of the galleries and public spaces.
item.dc:creator: John Hanc
item.media:credit: Daniel Terna for The New York Times

item.title: What to See This Spring at Museums Across the U.S.
item.description: The season includes a Duchamp retrospective at MoMA, a window on Etruscan civilization at the Legion of Honor in San Francisco and a fashion celebration at the Phoenix Art Museum.
item.dc:creator: Morgan Malget
item.category: de Young, M H, Memorial Museum
New Britain Museum of American Art
Museum of Fine Arts, St Petersburg (Fla)

item.title: A New Exhibition at New York’s Natural History Museum Honors Fossil Hunters
item.description: In a new and ongoing exhibition, the American Museum of Natural History highlights the findings of Mark Norell and other fossil hunters responsible for its most important discoveries.
item.dc:creator: Mark A. Stein
item.media:credit: Chang W. Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Paleontologists and their team excavate an Ankylosaur, an armored dinosaur, at Camel’s Humps in the Gobi Desert in Mongolia in 2005.

item.title: At a Difficult Time, a Minnesota Museum Offers Respite to Somalis
item.description: As one of the few institutions of its kind in the world, the Somali Museum of Minnesota has become a center of the immigrant community.
item.dc:creator: Alex V. Cipolle
item.media:credit: Jenn Ackerman for The New York Times
item.media:description: The interior of a Somali hut in the Somali Museum of Minnesota. The dome is woven in an intricate diamond pattern from vibrant yarns, acacia tree fibers and dried grasses.

item.title: The Benefactor of the Pulitzer Arts Foundation Curates Its Collection
item.description: Emily Rauh Pulitzer, a veteran curator and collector, leans heavily on sculpture and drawing in a show of some 85 works at the Pulitzer Arts Foundation.
item.dc:creator: Ted Loos
item.media:credit: Neeta Satam for The New York Times

item.title: Adriano Goldschmied, ‘Godfather’ of Modern Denim, Dies at 82
item.description: An energetic entrepreneur, he developed more than 50 brands of jeans, including Gap 1969 and other cult classics.
item.dc:creator: Penelope Green

item.title: Route 66, a ‘Linear Museum Stretched Across Eight States,’ Turns 100
item.description: Institutions large and small examine the complicated history of the iconic corridor that helped define the American road trip.
item.dc:creator: Janelle Conaway
item.category: Albuquerque Museum of Art and History
item.media:credit: Anna Mardo/Getty Images
item.media:description: Although Route 66 is no longer an official U.S. highway, the road has gained traction in the popular imagination and has become a draw for national and international tourism.

item.title: Why Does the “Rocky” Statue Draw Crowds? This Show Investigates.
item.description: This spring, the Philadelphia Museum of Art invites the bronze boxer inside to center an exhibition on why we make monuments and what they mean.
item.dc:creator: Mekado Murphy
item.category: Monument Lab (Philadelphia, Pa, Public Art Studio)

item.title: San Francisco’s Modern Art Museum Reimagines the Fisher Collection
item.description: The Bay Area family made a deal with SFMOMA that called for exhibitions of the collection’s works every 10 years. Some 250 pieces are now showing.
item.dc:creator: Michael Janofsky
item.category: San Francisco Museum of Modern Art
item.media:credit: Anastasiia Sapon for The New York Times
item.media:description: “Geometric Apple Core” by Claes Oldenburg and Coosje van Bruggen is among the items in the exhibition “Reimagined: The Fisher Collection at 10.”

item.title: M.F.A. Boston Celebrates 50 Years of Flowers and Art
item.description: The annual “Art in Bloom” exhibition began in 1976 and has spawned similar events at other museums across the country.
item.dc:creator: Shivani Vora
item.category: Fine Arts Museums of San Francisco
item.media:credit: Neeta Satam for The New York Times
item.media:description: A floral arrangement by Aniyah Donner, inspired by “Bathers With a Turtle” by Henri Matisse, was seen during “Art in Bloom” 2026 at the Saint Louis Art Museum.

item.title: In Indianapolis, a New Contemporary Art Museum Comes With a D.J.
item.description: The 40,000-square-foot space, housed in a former dairy barn, aims to upend expectations of what an art museum can be.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr and Lee Klafczynski
item.media:description: From left, Sylvia Thomas, a poet, writer and musician; the sculptor Bryn Jackson; and the artist India Hines.
